title: Beeswax jar candle with lavender
subtitle: A slow-burning container candle with pure beeswax and lavender essential oil
excerpt: Beeswax burns slower and hotter than soy and brings its own subtle honey scent. This 200 ml jar candle uses 130 g of beeswax pellets with lavender essential oil at 4% of wax weight, keeping the lavender gentle against the wax's natural character. Burn time is 30 to 35 hours.
sourceNotes: Beeswax container candles: Candlescience and CandleScience Beeswax Candle Guide (2023). Beeswax melting point 62 to 65°C; pour temperature 68 to 72°C (higher than soy to avoid shrinkage). Fragrance load for beeswax: 3 to 5% is typical; higher rates can interfere with the beeswax crystalline structure and cause scent sweating. Bloom (white powdery film) is normal for beeswax and can be buffed off with a soft cloth.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 200 ml jar candle (130 g beeswax fill)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale by multiplying all weights by the number of jars. One pre-tabbed wick per jar.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for 3 to 5 days before burning. Bloom may form on the surface within the first few days; this is harmless.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Beeswax is stable for many years; the lavender essential oil fragrance fades after about 2 years. Bloom (a white powdery film) may appear on the surface; buff with a soft cloth before burning. Discard if the wick base corrodes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A white powdery or hazy film that forms on the surface of cooled beeswax. Caused by the migration of fine wax crystals to the surface as the wax stabilises. Harmless; buff off with a soft cloth or fingertip before burning.
The temperature at which liquid wax is poured into the container. Beeswax is poured at 68 to 72°C, higher than soy wax, to reduce shrinkage and adhesion problems.

content.text: Beeswax burns slower and hotter than soy, giving a longer burn. It has its own faint honey scent, so the lavender sits alongside it rather than filling empty air. Lavender at 4% of wax weight is gentle enough not to overpower the wax. Beeswax also tends to form
on the surface as it cools; this is normal and buffs off with a soft cloth. Pour temperature is higher than soy, so keep the thermometer to hand.

items.prepNote: Pure cosmetic or candle-grade beeswax pellets
CD-12 or ECO-8 for a 6 to 7 cm diameter jar; check manufacturer sizing guide for beeswax

content.text: Place the beeswax pellets in the candle pouring pitcher. Set in a pan of barely simmering water (a bain-marie). Heat, stirring occasionally, until fully melted. Beeswax melts at 62 to 65°C; heat to 75°C to ensure the wax is completely liquid. Remove from the heat.

While the wax cools, secure the sustainer tab of the pre-tabbed wick to the centre of the jar base with a dab of melted wax or a wick sticker. Thread the wick through the centring bar and lay the bar across the jar rim so the wick is centred and taut.
Add the essential oil and pour
When the beeswax has cooled to 65°C, stir in the lavender essential oil for 2 minutes. Pour at

: between 68 and 72°C. Beeswax poured too cool adheres poorly to the jar and can crack. Pour in a slow stream down the side of the jar, leaving a 5 to 10 mm gap at the top. Leave undisturbed until fully cooled.
Leave for at least 24 hours before the first burn. Bloom may form on the surface within the first few days; buff gently with a soft cloth before burning. Trim the wick to 5 mm before every burn.

Beeswax is one of the most stable natural waxes and keeps for many years. The lavender essential oil scent fades after about 2 years; the wax itself does not degrade. Keep the lid on between burns. Bloom is cosmetic only: wipe or buff before burning. Discard if the wick base corrodes.
